Yes, it's been corrupted by the enemy, but we that have been saved by the grace of God, God has given to us a new song, and I'm thankful for this gift of music, and I want the music that I sing, I want the music that I listen to, I want the music in our church to glorify God and to teach me, to minister to me. I want to be reminded of biblical truth when I'm singing songs. I want to be reminded of how great my God is when I'm singing songs. When you begin to think about how He is able to deliver thee, and you sing that song, and you're going through some difficulty and trial, and oh, how that song will minister to you. And it's interesting. I always find it interesting as we take the opportunity at different times during our calendar year to take for people to give testimonies and to share favorites. Just your favorite hymn, and we'll sing a verse of that favorite hymn. And it's really amazing to me to see how God uses songs in people's lives, to minister to them. Sometimes the song that you might say is your favorite this week or this month or this year, there might be a situation that you face, a different season in life, and God will use a different song that will become your favorite at that moment. Why? Because you deal with different things, and so do I, and God uses those songs. How often is it that many times in even a congregation the size of ours that you'll have people that will pick the same song as their favorite? I can almost guarantee every time that we take favorites that there's going to be a certain number of songs that will be included. Great is thy faithfulness. It's one that's like, it's going, somebody's going to pick it. It is well with my soul. Somebody's going to pick it. Amazing Grace, Brother Don is going to pick it. Amen? How many of you know that that's his favorite song? Listen, he said, pastor, when I have my funeral, I already know we're singing Amazing Grace. We're going to sing it 47 times. I got it. I understand, right? Why? It's his favorite song. Because God uses songs to minister to us, but we're to sing unto God, and it's a form of worship. We're to sing aloud. God's put a song in our heart and our mouth, and we need to sing praise to Him. Remember, Paul and Silas, they sang unto God, and the prisoners heard them. What an opportunity for them to be able to sing to their God in a very difficult circumstance to be ministered to, but also to have an impact on others. Praise with song. Let's pray. Father, we love You. We thank You for loving us. We thank You for the opportunity to be able to gather, and Lord, we thank You for the ministry of music, the gift of music, and God, I pray that You would help us to sing aloud unto our God, who is our strength, Lord, unto Thee. Lord, help us to praise You, to make a joyful noise. Lord, help us to have right music, have music that teaches us, that admonishes us, that reminds us and reinforces to us Bible truth. We thank You that we can pray together as a church family. We thank You that You're a God who... hears and answers. And I pray for these next moments, Lord, that You'd have Your will and way. For it's in Christ's name we pray. Amen. All right, Brother Fernando.

segments.text: nights through this psalm. We did Psalm 25 for three weeks and then got into Psalm 81. And if
you were here last Wednesday evening, we actually really didn't do a whole lot of anything in Psalm
81. We gave an introduction to the psalm and understood that this particular psalm is a psalm
that was associated with the Feast of Tabernacles. And so for us to have a greater understanding
of this psalm as we would study through it, I thought it necessary to go back and to study
in the book of Leviticus and reference some stuff over the book of Exodus as well with regard to
the Feast of the Tabernacles. We also went over the New Testament and saw the Lord Jesus Christ
in John 7 there and so on and so forth, and introductory lessons so that we could gather
some understanding of this particular psalm. So this evening, we'll begin looking at the psalm,
looking at the first three verses this evening. Maybe you've heard it said, it's been said often
that the book of Psalms being referred to as Israel's songbook. And certainly there's truth
to that. Indeed, even still today, there are particular psalms or parts of psalms that we
still sing today. We probably don't sing it the same way that they did. Sometimes we look at it
and someone's put it to music and we'll sing it. And some of you know some of the psalms
that we'll sing. Psalm 48, great is the Lord. Psalm 19, the law of the Lord is perfect. And
there's several of them, just a couple of them quickly. But we know that this book has often
been referred to as Israel's songbook. And I'm thankful for the ministry of music and for the
gift of music. I would say this, that music is a gift from God to humanity. And we are, as humans,
we are musical people. And music ministers to the heart. And as we begin looking at this particular
psalm in these first three verses, we see this aspect of singing. Notice it says, sing aloud
unto God our strength, make a joyful noise unto the God of Jacob. Take a psalm and bring hither
the timbrel and pleasant harp with the psaltery. Blow up the trumpet in the new moon, the time
appointed on our solemn feast day. As we look at these three verses, I've simply entitled the
message for this evening as we get into Psalm 81, praise with song. And music, as I mentioned
already, is a gift from God to humanity. It's a means by which we are able to praise the Lord.
You think about the ministry of music, the purpose of music for us as we begin to look at
the content of Psalm 81, and even thinking about within the context of church and as believers,
the ministry of music. And I would say that music is part of life and culture. And you can go all
around the world and you can find music. It's very interesting. In fact, I would say this, I love
music. But you know something interesting, and you might be able to say the same thing,
I love music today, but I loved music before I got saved. It was a different kind of music,
but I love music. And the reality is that when I got saved, my music changed, but we are musical
beings. Music has great power, the ability of those that are writing songs to send forth a
message, not only through the lyrics of the songs, but also, of course, through the beat of the song,
etc., etc. And so we see some of those things. I'm not going to dive into all of that this evening,
but we understand that we're musical beings. Think about children. I mean, just little kids
in a nursery, you start singing songs and they respond to that. They are drawn to that. Why?
Because of this music. Why is it that so many of these videos and things that are so prevalent
today with regard to children and the music that is there, and just it's obvious that music is part
of our nature as human beings. I think about this, and we'll be in Psalm 81, but look over at Psalm 40
if you will. And I want you to notice what the psalmist says in Psalm 40, and we see a very
important truth because we speak about being musical people. And I shared with you already
that I loved music before I got saved, but my music has changed. And notice in Psalm 40, in
verse number one, I waited patiently for the Lord, and he inclined unto me and heard my cry. He
brought me up also out of a horrible pit, out of the miry clay, and set my feet upon a rock,
and established my goings. But notice in verse number three, and he hath put a new song in my mouth.
Even praise unto our God, many shall see it, and fear, and shall trust in the Lord. I would say
this, and you'd have to answer for yourself, but I would say that the music that I loved
before I got saved was not music that was praised to God. It wasn't. But it was music that I liked,
that I enjoyed. But after I got saved, God put a new song in my mouth, and God changed that aspect.
And I love music. I don't always sing on tune. We joke about it sometimes, about which key I'm going
to be in, and what happens when there's a key change. And I remember distinctly one time,
a few years ago, when we had a choir, and I was singing in the choir, and they decided that
it needed to be in a different key. And Miss Kimberly left this piano to go over here to this
one, so that it would transpose and put it in a different key. And so I asked for new music,
and Pierre looked at me like, what do you mean you need new music? I was like, well, if it's in a new
key, I got to have it, because this music is in that key. And I just, I don't understand everything
about music, but I love music. God's put a song in our heart. And you notice in Psalm 81,
that music is how this psalm begins. We mentioned last week about the Feast of Tabernacles,
and the aspect of praise, and what they would do, and how they were rejoicing for the harvest,
and what have you. And you see where the scriptures begin in this psalm with this
emphasis upon singing. Music is to praise God. It's to minister to our hearts. It's not
to entertain. That's not the purpose of music. We find that when we have special music in church,
when we sing hymns, and so on and so forth, when we speak about church music, there are times in
which people will give a hearty amen to a song, maybe that's being done with a special music,
that sort of thing. Maybe, as it happened, I think, two weeks ago, there were several people
that clapped at the end of a song. But that's not the purpose. It's not entertainment. It's
ministering. It's ministering to our hearts. There are times, I think it was even this past week,
that we put the words to the song up on the screen. Why? So that you can see the words. You
can read the words as the song is being sung. Why? So that it can minister to you. So it can minister
to me. And what a blessing to think about the ministry of music. As we begin to look in Psalm
81, and we learn some things about music, we notice that there are some truths here that God
gives us that are important. The first thing that we recognize is answering this question,
to whom do we sing? We find very plainly in verse number one, the Bible says, sing aloud unto God.
Sing aloud unto God our strength. And it continues, and make a joyful noise unto the God
of Jacob. They were singing to God. By the way, we have someone, and we have something to sing
about. Amen? Aren't you thankful when you sing a song about praising the Lord, living for Jesus?
We just sang it this evening. What a great hymn that reminds us of what he did for us, and how
we're to live for him. And as we sing the song, we're not just filling time. We're not just trying
to, we have to have this service schedule, and it has to be this way, and you have to include
this number of songs. No, it's ministering to us. The song service is part of worship.
Amen? The song service is not intended to be the time in which we're mingling, and talking,
and doing this, and checking this. And no, it's a time in which we're singing, and we're praising
God. And we're not only praising God, but through the song, as God would minister to our hearts,
and prepare us for the message that would follow. And we find that here, the Bible makes it very
plain that we are singing unto God. The psalmist is not giving instruction with regard to those
that would be gathered, that you're singing to them as being your primary audience. And yes,
when somebody sings here, there's an audience, the congregation, but we're singing to God.
And when you're singing to God, it makes all the difference. And we find this evening that
he makes it very plain that God is our strength, and he says at the end of verse number one,
unto the God of Jacob. And we understand, as we mentioned last week, this being a psalm that would
be relevant, and associated with the Feast of Tabernacles, and the emphasis there with being
unto the God of Jacob. Jacob, of course, becoming Israel, and so on and so forth.
But we find this evening that when we sing, we learn
learn some truth from Psalm 81 that we're singing to God.

unto God our strength, make a joyful noise unto the God of Jacob. And it tells

This evening I'm thankful that God saved me and that God put a new song in my mouth. And
I'm thankful today that my music is not the same music that I used to listen to. And my
music, listen, I want to listen to music that reminds me of how great my God is. I
want to listen to music that challenges me in my walk with God. And when I have, if you
were to sit in my office with me and you were to listen to some of the songs that I listen
to, they're songs that are doing exactly what I just said. They're reminding me of how wonderful
my God is and what He's done for me. And it's, man, it's just encouraging me and it's
ministering to me and it's strengthening me and it's just provoking me to go and to live
for Jesus. Ephesians 5, the Bible says this in verse number 18, and be not drunk with
wine or in his excess, but be filled with the Spirit. Now it's always interesting to
me that many times people say, I'm filled with the Spirit, but the Bible gives us really
evidences of what it means to be filled with the Spirit. Now we understand that we're commanded
to be filled with the Spirit. Being indwelled by the Spirit is what happens at the moment
of salvation. When I get saved, when you got saved, or when I said when I get, when I got
saved and when you got saved, when we trust that Christ is our Savior, the Holy Spirit
of God indwelled us. But the idea of the filling of the Holy Spirit of God is something
that changes. Why? Because we change. We're filled with self and we need to be emptied
of self. And just because I'm filled with the Spirit today doesn't mean I'm going to
be filled with the Spirit tomorrow. Just because I'm filled with the Spirit at 7.30 doesn't
mean I'm going to be filled with the Spirit at 8 o'clock, right? You're tracking. And
so we find that He says, but be filled with the Spirit. And here's some of the evidence.
Just like when you look at Galatians, when it talks about the fruit of the Spirit is,
don't say that you're walking in the Spirit, but there's no love, joy, peace, longsuffering.
No, that's the evidence. And here the Bible says, speaking to yourselves in psalms and
hymns, and it also says in spiritual songs, singing and making melody in your heart to
the Lord. Giving thanks always for all things unto God and the Father in the name of our
Lord Jesus Christ. You know, sometimes you might want to say, does it really have to
say always? Does it really have to say all things? Yes, because God is good all the time.
Amen? And He's worthy of our praise. We don't praise Him because our circumstances are good
all the time. We praise Him because He's good all the time. And it says, submitting yourselves
one to another in the fear of God. And so we find that in the context in which the Apostle
Paul is encouraging the believers in Ephesus. He's already established in the first three
chapters the fact that they're saved and their position is secure, is saved people. But now,
as he gets into chapter four and following, he's dealing with that practical aspect of what does
it mean now that you're a Christian? How do you live? How do you walk? The word walk is used
repeatedly in Ephesians 4, 5, and 6 because we're to walk a certain way because we've been saved.
And so when he talks about being filled with the Spirit, he lists these evidences and he lists
what is this going to look like? And it's interesting that verse 19 immediately starts
dealing with this subject of singing, of music. Speaking to yourselves in psalms and hymns and
spiritual songs, singing and making melody in your heart to who? To the Lord. To the Lord.
And what a blessing it is to be able to praise God through song. You know, just to be able to
have God remind you of a song and just allow it to minister to your heart as you go through maybe
a time of difficulty, as you go through whatever it is that you're facing and you're just, man,
God just ministers through song. I've mentioned this before and I think that it's essential to
understand and I don't claim to be a music scholar in any way, shape, or form. There's
much that I do not know about music and I readily admit that. But I do understand that we're all
different people and that when you start talking about right music, wrong music, that there's
really an idea of being able to look at a spectrum, if you will. And so there's right
music that might not be your style of music. Does that make sense tonight? It's not that it's wicked
music, it's still good music, it's still God-honoring music, but it may not be the exact
style that you like. In fact, if you were to think about some of, even some folks that have had the
opportunity to sing in our church, I think about when we had Pierre and Michael and Joseph and Tim,
I think, sang that four-men quartet. It was, that style of that was different than when some people
sing just a solo. But it doesn't mean that one's right and one's wrong. It's a different style. I
think they're both right. Again, if you could think about it linearly, like a spectrum, and
there's a section that, yes, this is bad, wrong music, this is bad, wrong music, but there's this
area that's going to be, this is really my style of music. This is really my style of music. I'm
sure you've heard, I remember hearing it years ago, of people comparing different Bible colleges
and their style of music. And that style of music from that Bible college is different than the style
of music from this Bible college. Are they saying that one's right and one's wrong? Well, some do
go that far to say that. I think if you go to some places, not only in the U.S., but even if you go to
some places around the world, you're going to find slightly different style of music. But it doesn't
necessarily mean it's wrong music. It still can be God-honoring, Christ-honoring music. In fact,
I think I've been to Israel. I know some of you have. The style of music in Israel is different
than the style of music that we have here in America. It just is. It's just different style
of music. The style of music in Kentucky? I'm not a bluegrass fan. Never was, right? But you
understand, there's just a different style. And I'm not, I would not say that just because the style
isn't what you say is your style, it doesn't mean it's wrong. There are some things that you can
look to, to be able to determine if something's right or wrong, and there are tests that we can
put forth, but we must be mindful of the fact that there's going to be within this spectrum,
if you will, that, okay, that's the style that I really like, but that's maybe a style that I
don't like. And it's important that we understand that there's an aspect of grace with that. And
there are lines in which you can go outside one side or the other, and it cross the line and be,
okay, this is not appropriate music. Yeah, but the words, speaking to yourselves in songs and
hymns and spiritual songs, singing and making melody in your hearts to the Lord. Again, when
you start getting into melody and harmony and all that sort of thing, I get kind of like, I'm
checked out, it's beyond my ability. But there's certainly an aspect that you can't just say, well,
just listen to the words. The words have to be appropriate, but there's other aspects of the
music that have to be appropriate as well. And so we have to understand that, but we also have
to have a measure of grace with that. And then look over at Colossians 3, if you will. We look
at Colossians 3, and notice what the Bible teaches us here as we continue to understand this aspect
of praise God with song and how they were given the responsibility in the opening verses of Psalm
81 to sing, to sing aloud unto God our God.
our strength, to make a joyful noise unto the God of Jacob."
